hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (973mg/100g), exceeding the 600mg/100g threshold; high saturated fat contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Edam cheese is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension due to its very high sodium content of 973mg/100g; if consumed at all, limit to a single thin slice (10–15g) on an occasional basis and pair with potassium-rich foods such as tomato or avocado to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (1.43g/100g) and zero sugar pose no glycaemic risk, but high saturated fat (18g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance and high sodium may complicate diabetes-related cardiovascular risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Edam cheese does not raise blood glucose due to its negligible carbohydrate content, making it glycaemically safe in small portions; however, its high saturated fat content (approximately 18g/100g) may impair insulin sensitivity over time, and its very high sodium (973mg/100g) elevates cardiovascular risk in people with Type 2 Diabetes — limit to one slice (28g) per occasion and choose lower-sodium dairy alternatives where possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (approximately 18g/100g) raises LDL cholesterol; very high sodium (973mg/100g) elevates blood pressure; cholesterol content 89mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Edam cheese poses a high cardiovascular concern due to its combination of high saturated fat (~18g/100g), very high sodium (973mg/100g), and dietary cholesterol (89mg/100g); individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional very small portions (10–15g) and prioritise lower-fat, lower-sodium dairy alternatives such as low-fat cottage cheese or ricotta.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~18g/100g) and naturally occurring trans fat (~0.3g/100g from dairy) promote pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Edam cheese is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high saturated fat content (~18g/100g) and naturally occurring trans fat (~0.3g/100g), both of which promote systemic inflammation; if calcium intake is a concern, lower-fat dairy sources such as low-fat yogurt or fortified plant-based alternatives are preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Edam cheese is a semi-hard Dutch cheese originating from the town of Edam in the Netherlands. It is made by curdling pasteurised cow's milk with rennet, pressing the curds into a spherical or loaf shape, brining for salt absorption, and then aging — typically for a minimum of four weeks for young Edam and up to 17 months for aged varieties. The cheese has a smooth, firm texture that becomes harder and more crumbly with age. Its flavour is mild and slightly salty when young, developing a sharper, nuttier profile as it matures. Edam is traditionally coated in red paraffin wax for export, which is its most recognisable visual feature. Nutritionally, Edam cheese is energy-dense at 357 kcal per 100g, with high protein (25g/100g) and high fat (28.57g/100g), of which approximately 18g is saturated fat. Carbohydrate content is negligible at 1.43g/100g with no sugar, making it glycaemically inert. Sodium is very high at 973mg/100g, which is a significant clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Calcium content is exceptionally high at 731mg/100g, making it a valuable source of dietary calcium. Edam is commonly used in sandwiches, cheese boards, melted on dishes, or eaten as a snack. It is widely available in Singapore supermarkets and is popular in Western-style meals and snack platters.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01010014, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient values cross-referenced and confirmed against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Edam cheese (FDC ID 173420), an approved secondary source. Both sources are consistent and approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Sugar_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): HPB database explicitly reports 0g sugar per 100g for Edam cheese, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (naturally zero): cheese contains no plant-derived fibre. Saturated fat (18.0g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (7.93g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.67g/100g), trans_fat_g (0.3g/100g), and all micronutrient values not reported by HPB were sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173420) for Edam cheese — an approved secondary source. Vitamin_c_mg set to -1: Edam cheese contains negligible or no vitamin C; USDA SR Legacy reports 0mg but HPB does not report this field, and the value is below meaningful detection for this food type; set to -1 to indicate not meaningfully reported rather than confirmed zero. Trans fat of 0.3g/100g is naturally occurring dairy trans fat (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid), not industrially produced; this is documented in USDA SR Legacy data. Halal status is not confirmed as rennet source (animal vs microbial) is not specified in HPB or USDA data — marked false pending verification. Edam cheese; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (1.43g/100g). The glycemic_index field is set to the numeric value 0 per template structure requirements; gi_category 'not_applicable' is the authoritative classification for this food. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0.
